race.name: Tour de Brisbane
race.tagline: Australia's ultimate closed-road gran fondo through a vibrant city on fully shut streets

vitals.field_size: Open to all ages and abilities; competitive age-group categories in 5-year brackets
vitals.registration: Includes free event jersey for select distances, timing chip, race pack; AusCycling membership recommended for insurance
vitals.cutoff_time: Road closure curfew points enforced for fully closed road compliance
route_options: 110km (68.35 mi), ~4,068 ft — UCI Gran Fondo qualifier with age-group competition and Worlds qualification
80km (49.7 mi), ~2,257 ft — Mid-distance option for intermediate cyclists
50km (31.1 mi), ~1,682 ft — Accessible entry point for recreational riders
4km (~2.5 mi) — Big Family Ride community event for children 8+ with adult supervision

climate.primary: Subtropical — warm and generally favorable for cycling
climate.description: Brisbane in April offers ideal cycling conditions with warm temperatures and manageable weather patterns. The subtropical climate means minimal rain risk during autumn, though humidity remains moderate. Year-round cycling conditions make Brisbane a cyclist-friendly destination.

terrain.primary: Urban road course on fully closed city streets with one significant climb
terrain.surface: Fully closed roads through Brisbane landmarks and city infrastructure. The course features urban pavement, expressways, tunnels, and bridges with technical turns integrated into the city layout. Mount Coot-tha provides the signature elevation challenge at 2km, 9% gradient.

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 64. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 64.

course_description.character: Tour de Brisbane transforms urban infrastructure into a cycling playground, starting at City Botanic Gardens and weaving through Brisbane's heart on fully closed roads. The course loops through the city center, crosses the Brisbane River via major bridges, navigates through tunnels and expressways, and features five technical turns requiring constant attention. Mount Coot-tha provides the only significant climbing challenge, while the remainder emphasizes urban navigation, tactical positioning, and the unique experience of riding through normally car-filled streets. The finish returns to South Bank Cultural Precinct, completing a comprehensive tour of Brisbane's cycling-friendly infrastructure.
course_description.signature_challenge: Mount Coot-tha's 2km climb at 9% gradient serves as the race's defining moment, arriving at 67km to determine final positioning. While modest by gran fondo standards, the climb's placement late in the event and its role as the course's only significant elevation gain make it tactically crucial. The summit's panoramic Brisbane views reward the effort before the final descent to South Bank finish.

logistics.airport: Brisbane Airport (BNE) is approximately 15km from the city center with direct international connections. Ground transportation via taxi, rideshare, or rental car is straightforward. The airport's proximity to accommodation and event venues makes arrival logistics simple.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Stay in Brisbane's city center (South Bank, CBD, or Fortitude Valley) for walking distance to start/finish and post-race dining. Budget options exist alongside luxury hotels. Booking 2-3 months in advance recommended for April availability.
logistics.food: Brisbane offers excellent dining across all price points, with particular strength in Asian cuisine and contemporary Australian cooking. The South Bank Cultural Precinct and riverside restaurants provide pre/post-race dining options. Local craft breweries and cafes support cycling culture.

history.origin_story: Tour de Brisbane launched as Australia's first UCI Gran Fondo World Series event, introducing the closed-road gran fondo concept to Australian cycling. The event emerged from Brisbane's growing cycling culture and desire to create a world-class cycling celebration accessible to all abilities. Integration with the broader Brisbane Cycling Festival positioned it as a destination event combining sport, community, and urban exploration. The 2025 edition relocated the start to newly built Queens Wharf, demonstrating continued evolution and investment in event infrastructure.
notable_moments: 2019: Inaugural Tour de Brisbane establishes Australia's first UCI Gran Fondo World Series event
2025: Start relocated to Queens Wharf with finish on Victoria Bridge, demonstrating infrastructure investment
2026: Introduction of free event jerseys for 120km, 80km, and 50km entries
Ongoing: Charity fundraising partnership with The Prince Charles Hospital Foundation for heart and lung research
history.reputation: Tour de Brisbane has established itself as a well-organized, community-focused cycling event with legitimate UCI credentials. The event is respected within Australian cycling circles and increasingly recognized internationally as a quality gran fondo destination. Its emphasis on accessibility and inclusion distinguishes it from more elite-focused events.

biased_opinion.summary: Tour de Brisbane represents a genuinely innovative approach to gran fondo racing—taking the closed-road concept into a vibrant city rather than rural mountains. The event delivers on its promise of fully closed urban infrastructure, excellent logistics, and strong community engagement. However, it's fundamentally a different animal than traditional gran fondos: flatter, shorter, and more about urban cycling experience than climbing or distance. For Australian cyclists or those seeking a unique international cycling destination, it's compelling. For riders chasing elevation, prestige, or classic gran fondo suffering, look elsewhere.
strengths: Fully closed urban roads create genuinely unique cycling experience
UCI Gran Fondo World Series qualifier with legitimate Worlds qualification pathway
Inclusive community focus with multiple distance options and family participation
Excellent April weather and year-round cycling climate
weaknesses: Modest elevation gain (4,068 ft) lacks climbing challenge
Moderate distance (110km) doesn't test endurance like epic gran fondos
Limited historical prestige compared to established European events
Uncertain competitive field depth and elite participation
International travel costs substantially increase total event expense
biased_opinion.bottom_line: Tour de Brisbane succeeds brilliantly at what it attempts—a well-executed, community-focused urban cycling celebration with legitimate UCI credentials. It's not a traditional gran fondo and shouldn't be evaluated as one. This is a destination event for cyclists seeking unique experience and Australian cycling culture.

final_verdict.one_liner: Innovative urban gran fondo with excellent logistics but modest climbing and prestige.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you value unique urban cycling experiences, seek UCI Worlds qualification, or want to explore Brisbane's cycling culture. Skip if you're chasing serious elevation, classic gran fondo suffering, or established prestige—this isn't that event.
final_verdict.alternatives: For elevation-focused gran fondos, consider Gravel Worlds or Belgian Waffle Ride. For established European prestige, try Giro di Lombardia or L'Étape du Tour. For accessible urban cycling events, explore Gran Fondo New York.

videos.title: How I Qualified for the Gran Fondo World Championship // Tour de Brisbane 2025
videos.description:
I travelled to Queensland to race the Tour de Brisbane in an attempt to qualify for the Gran Fondo World Championship. Spoiler alert: I made it! Checkout a full race recap from what was a super fast, and dangerously sketchy day.

🚴‍♂️ Tour de Brisbane 118km 1237m elevation done and dusted in 3 hours! https://www.strava.com/activities/14160322198

🏷️ #Cycling #RoadCycling #TourdeBrisbane

Footage from: @chrismillercycling @tourdebrisbane2673 @itsmaxmann

🚰 Nutrition support from Precision Fuel & Hydration - https://www.precisionhydration.com/au/en/

💨 Get yourself an electric pump. Better than C02 - CYCPLUS AS2 Tiny E-Pump (referral link) - https://www.cycplus.com/?ref=hfrkcicm

🎵 Find all the tracks from all my videos in this handy playlist (Referral link) https://share.epidemicsound.com/8lgfzh/?playlist=xlf2mzt3lywqdnhq9dxdo5lbgejd9v11

👉🏻 Follow me on Instagram and Strava
➡️ https://www.instagram.com/moderatecyclist/
➡️ https://www.strava.com/athletes/bkpaton

00:00 - Introduction
01:18 - Race Start
02:03 - The Course
04:21 - Nutrition
07:30 - Bike Check
08:24 - Mt Cootha Climb
10:23 - Kit Choices
12:05 - Race Finish
12:33 - Wrap-up
videos.transcript:
I set myself a goal for this year to
qualify for the Grand Fondo World
Championships because it is in Australia
and it would be an amazing experience to
be involved in something so grand. I
missed Amy's Grand Fondo last year, so
tour to Brisbane was my last chance. If
I was going to make this happen, I'd
have to fly to Brisbane. But before
that, I'd have to track down a travel
bag and get over the anxiety of flying
with a bike. Based on my retro recon, I
was going to need to finish in the top
50 riders of my age group to make the
25% cut. This would mean completing the
course in three hours or less and
adopting a race to win mentality, which
is totally new to me. Talk down your
bolts and top up your tires. I'll take
you through the course, my nutrition
kit, and bike along the way. But let's
not waste any more time. Let's get the
race underway.
[Music]
Something like 7,000 people made getting
to the start line absolute chaos.
Fortunately, there were a few familiar
faces. How you feeling? Good, mate. How
you feeling? Getting it. The advice I'd
been given was to get to the front, even
if it meant being a jerk. I'd set my
mark on Brad Banister, who I knew was a
good shot for a podium, if not a win
today. If I could stick to his wheel, I
was going to be in the right position.
My overall plan of attack was pretty
simple. Hold the front pack until the
climb, which is about 70 km in. Aim for
around about 5 W per kilo on the climb
and hope to crest with a group who will
work to seal the deal. Other than that,
the rain added one more plan and that
was to stay upright. I managed to get
within a few wheels from the front. So,
this was a great position to be starting
the race from. I latched on to Brad's
wheel and everything was going.
I had done a brief Straa recon of the
course, but I'm not super familiar with
Brisbane, so for the greatest part, this
was going to be a bit of a mystery tour.
What was pretty clear from early on
though, it was going to be a pretty
gnarly day. This is probably a great
opportunity to talk about the course
itself. It's a pretty amazing course, a
true Metropolitan Grand Fondo. It
showcases Brisbane City, its parks, and
the bridges across the river. It is
linked together by motorways and
tunnels. It is a total distance of 120
km with around about 1,700 m elevation.
Easy enough, right? Unfortunately, in
reality, it was a little more
complex. Rounding through a capital city
and juggling logistics of thousands of
riders across multiple distances and age
groups made for some pretty sketchy,
twisty sections littered with road
furniture. I will call this part of the
course the danger zone. Other than that,
there were three outand backs on closed
motorways. Let's call them the
speedways. Then a loop in the middle
containing the only climb and primary
selection point. The Mount Cuther climb
is only about 2.5 km with an average
gradient of 7%. It's going to be an
allout effort. We'll call this the hurt
circle. Escaping the danger zone for the
first time was the hardest of all. There
were some really gnarly sections and I
couldn't capture them all because I was
hanging on for dear life. I was
surprised how quickly we were catching
some of the other age groups. I couldn't
help but notice the body count piling up
and we were only 6 km in. At one point,
the rider to my right literally just
went to ground. I have no idea what
happened to him, but it didn't look
good. But we'd made it out alive and we
were on to the first speedway. These out
and back sections were long, flat, and
smooth. They ran in and out of tunnels
and through bus terminals and were
bookended by hairpin double backs. This
made it very hard to move around the
bunch, especially in the two-way
sections. In the days leading up to the
race, my daughter had tested positive
for influenza A. Although my own tests
were negative, I could feel the effects
of my immune response. Through the first
30 minutes, my heart rate was very high.
I knew that I needed to focus on keeping
it at a more manageable level. My plans
to follow the front markers were proving
more difficult than I expected. Not from
a fitness perspective, but from a pure
bunch logistics side. This is probably
my lack of experience coming in, but I
did spend a lot of time grinding it out
in the middle of the pelaton. While I
rumble my way through the bunch, let's
talk about my nutrition plan. My first
consideration was making sure I had
enough water for the duration of the
race. I picked up a couple of 950 mil
bottles. I filled these with precision
hydration drink mix, which made for a
total of 120 g of carbs, plus the
allimportant electrolytes. My primary
source of carbs would be a full 300 g
pouch of flow gel. This is a product
I've really come to love, especially for
its simplicity. I'll be running it in
the flask again, which is just so
convenient. I'll be supplementing this
with a couple of caffeine gels. This is
to help finish getting the morning carbs
in, but also to give me a little kick
and to substitute the coffees I'd
normally be drinking at this time of
day. All up, it gave me access to like
200 g per hour, which is way more than
necessary, but it gave me optionality in
the way that I took it on. You know you
were back in the danger zone when you
passed through what seems to be a
designated crash sign. This one had
ambulances waiting and a lineup of
bodies each time we passed through
it. At 35k in we're about to hit three
of the most questionable conclusions on
this course. Firstly, a trip over Green
Bridge. I can appreciate the value of
this leg for the casual riders. It was
very picturesque. But for the races, it
was another hairpin and quite a narrow
two-directional traffic in and out. It
was a lot of added risk for not much
more than about 1.5 km. Next was the
trip through Hill End or West End. Not
sure exactly what you would call it.
This was a very urban section of the
course and it was littered with road
furniture. It was a small climb, but it
didn't force a selection because
everyone was pretty rested after the
previous detour. There was a brief
moment where I thought the whole Pelaton
was going to come down, but we just
managed to scrape our way through. This
was the only one I managed to get on
camera, but this was not the only
touchandgo moment through here. The
third odd choice was when the road
narrowed and led us into the Riverside
Park. This was literally a park path,
which was single file at most times. To
make matters worse, the riders on the
front obviously had a clear run and were
trying to capitalize in forcing gap,
which only made it faster than was
comfortable on the available terrain. In
more than one location, there were
cables across the path with speed bump
covers over the top of them, which
caught riders by surprise. Ultimately,
we all ended up crushed into the exit to
get back onto the road. Anyway, after
all these shenanigans, we were back on
the speedway on our way out of the Dana
zone for our second out and back. The
push from the front didn't end, though.
The pressure to snap the elastic was
strong. Firstly on the bridges in the
city and then on the tunnels in the way.
This section was really intense and it
was the first time that I was worried
that I was not going to be able to hold
the wheel. But like magic when we
reached the end, everyone sat up a
little bit and it was a much more
manageable base. But the damage was
done. The front bunch had been thinned
just in time for it to get messy. The
integration with the other age groups
was starting and it was getting hard to
know exactly where you were in the pack.
I could still see my marks, so I knew I
was where I needed to be. While I catch
my breath, this is a great opportunity
to look at my bike
setup. I'm riding a Factor Ostro VAM,
old one, not new one in a size 58.
Durase 5236 on the front and 1130 on the
back. Wheels are blacking 60s with
tubeless
GP5000s. power meter via Wahoo Power
Play pedals. I'm running a Garmin 1050
computer. With all of this saddle bag
and two GoPros, the total weight is 8.74
kilograms. I've got to say a huge thanks
to the team at height cycling who hooked
me up with the bike travel bag. They
were super helpful and guided me through
the process. Be sure to pop in and check
out their showroom full of Italian bike
candy. We double back again and this is
where the first move started. We are now
on our way back to Mount Cuther, the
only climb. And there are a few shouts
of break from the pelaton, but no one
was having it. There were too many in
the group and the speed was too high.
There is no way anyone was getting away
here. In not too long, we hit the exit
for Mount Cuther and immediately the
power starts to ratchet up. Mount Cuther
starts with a solid 10% section which
immediately cuts the bunch in half. I
found myself on the back of the front
bunch. Initially, they were pulling
away, but as we rounded the first
corner, I was able to hold the gap
steady. My plan was not to hold their
wheel, but to pick up the riders who
fell off and form the second bunch. I
was confident this would secure the top
50 spot I needed if we could work and
hold off the stragglers when we hit the
flat. Everything was going to plan until
I hit the small flat section in the
middle of the climb. I took a quick
moment off the gas to regroup ahead of
the final pinch. Next minute, I dropped
my chain. No amount of gear changes were
getting it back on. I was going to have
to stop and put it on manually. These
were possibly the longest 15 seconds of
the whole day. Just lucky it didn't
happen on the really steep section, I
guess. But I got straight back on the
gas and I'd only lost around five places
of which I'd gained on the climb
previously. I had a bunch of about five
ahead of me. I just needed to catch
their wheel before it flattened out.
From here, the climb drags on for a fair
while past the so-called top. The front
side, which everybody talks about, is
2.1 km, but the full climb to the top
top is actually 6.3 km. The first 2 km
are very steep, but the back 4 km are
still fairly undulating, especially on
tide legs. We crested the official K
with a strung out bunch of about eight
who all seemed motivated to work after
the descent. I was fairly reserved on
the descent. I'm still regaining
confidence, especially in the wet after
having a crash earlier in the year.
Although I still managed to hit 80 km an
hour down the steep path. That is pretty
wild. Back towards the speedway and time
to regroup. Let's take a second to do a
quick fit check. Pretty standard
inclusions here for bibs and jersey.
Attacker Race 3.0 for both. This kit is
becoming an absolute mainstay in my
wardrobe. If only Attacker could keep it
in stock. One new addition is Aeros
socks from Fingers Crossed. It was only
a matter of time before I bought into
the hype. And rounded out with Oakley
Sutro and Car Utopia. Back on the
speedway, we had another flyby of the
danger zone before the last out and
back. We'd formed a good group. We knew
there were maybe 20 riders up the road
in the front bunch which would leave us
in a qualifying position. We just needed
to hang on to it. We got some turns
rolling and we started a motor quickly
picking up bunches as we went. Coming
through a tunnel, I heard my name and
turned to see it was Max Man, a fellow
YouTuber and friend of mine. He started
the day with the 19 to35 group. So I
knew that something had gone wrong for
him. If you want to see exactly what
happened to him, I'll link it up above.
But I also knew that he would jump into
the chop off and bring some extra
firepower to our bunch. At the pace we
were moving, our bunch grew pretty
quickly with a lot of sandbaggers, which
complicated the pace line, but the
serious lads kept it together, putting
in the big white turns. The last 20 km
threw everything at us. First, it
started to hammer down rain. It didn't
last long, but there was enough water on
the road that the wheel spray made it
feel like it was still raining. I
started to cramp in the hamstrings as we
doubled back from the airport. And we
had one more trip through the danger
zone before we would reach the finish
line. Now, the finish line was the last
curious part of the course. The rers's
guide suggested that the finish was on
the bridge. This would see us come out
of the bus tunnel and set up for a
sprint, but this is not quite how it
played out. As we emerged from the
tunnels, slightly blinded by the light
before I could even start to ramp up the
power, we were met by marshals telling
us to slow down. The race was over. We
had unknowingly crossed the finish line
as we exited the tunnel. It was a great
bunch of guys to finish with, all
incredibly strong. It's comforting to
know that these guys will be part of the
crew I'll be rolling with in the green
and gold later this year. I can't uncip
this thing for some reason. So, I'm
going to talk to you just here. But
damn, that was wild. We're like 120ks in
3 hours just wild. The rain was gnarly.
I don't know if I made it. We got to
wait and find out. Fingers crossed. That
drop chain that might have cost me the
day. In the end, it would seem I backed
the right horse. Brad came in second.
Congratulations to Michael in first and
Nick in third. As for me, I finished in
30th place, but most importantly, I got
myself one of these little cues, which
means I'm qualified for the Grand Fondo
World
Championship. That's it. Thanks to Tour
to Brisbane and the UCI World Series.
Thank you Brisbane City for loaning us
your roads. Thank you to my Queensland
family for coming to watch. Did you ride
or race? Let me know about your day in
the comments. Congratulations to
everyone who qualified. I'll see you in
lawn. But other than that folks, until
next time, see you.
videos.curation_reason: Excellent first-person race recap with detailed course breakdown and qualification story from 2025 edition.

videos.title: What REALLY Happens at Tour De Brisbane's UCI Qualifier?
videos.description:
Dive into the rollercoaster of a race that was Tour De Brisbane UCI Worlds Qualifier! With rain-soaked roads to start, the race immediately turned chaotic, with crashes littering the course. What should have been a straightforward qualification attempt quickly transformed into something else entirely...

The numbers don't lie: 38 kph average speed over 3 hours with a normalized power of 330W pushed me to new limits and even earned some unexpected PRs and Strava cups along the way. When cramps threatened to derail everything at the 65km mark, a trusty cramp fix saved the day. And when fatigue started creeping in? That's when the nootropics gel made its heroic appearance (if you know, you know - these things are absolute game-changers).

The infamous climb at 75km that had been living rent-free in my head for weeks actually felt manageable despite the lactic acid build-up. As we hit the 100km mark, the previously dried roads suddenly gave way to an absolute downpour. Nothing quite like chopping off with a bunch of randoms in the wet.

Despite all the challenges this UCI Qualifier threw our way, it was the kind of hurt that feels good - pushing boundaries and finding new levels within myself. Was it enough to punch that ticket to UCI Worlds? You'll have to watch to find out what went down!

One thing's certain: I'll be back next year for more Tour De Brisbane chaos.

Hope you enjoy the video!

videos.transcript:
Do you enjoy waking up before 5:00 a.m.
to go riding in the rain with thousands
around you and a high possibility of a
crash? That sounds like a banging day
out if you ask me. From the get-go, we
were already hundreds of riders back and
had to make some serious ground.
Thankfully, we did manage to avoid all
the crashes. However, we did run into a
few nightmares on the way. And the
question I was asking myself all day is,
can I still qualify for UCI World? To
find out the answer to that question,
you're going to have to watch the video
just a little bit longer. This ride was
absolute carnage. We averaged 330
normalized watts for the entirety of the
ride, which was 3 hours long. We found
ourselves chopping off through the
tunnels of Brisbane City, trying to
chase a good friend's wheel up a climb
and slowly getting dropped. And finally,
we make it to the end of the race.
However, the question was still
unanswered. Will we qualify for UCI
[Music]
World?
Morning.
Wait. We're on our way into town and
it's been raining the last couple days.
So, it's pretty pretty soaked down here
and the roads are very moist. So, until
the sun comes out, it's going to be bit
of a disaster. We're heading into to
Brisbane.
It's not going to last. It's not arrow,
bro. Take it off. Bit wet. It's not
looking good.
Hopefully the roads dry
up. Should be a banger day on the bike.
See if we can qualify.
All happening down here. We're in
amongst it and we are waiting for the
event to start. There's riders
everywhere. Victor's already in the
small train ring. He's ready for Cther.
Slowly shuffling forward. Apparently
nerves are kicking in now. What are you
going to say, bro? He just stay next to
the car. Will we qualify for UCI World?
I think look, he just stay next to the
car and everyone here. He He's here.
What is he doing? I don't know what
Victor is on
about.
Heat. Heat.
So, straight out of the gates, Tim and I
were on a mission to close down the gap
to the front group. If you didn't notice
at the start, we started relatively far
back and the front pack was quite a way
ahead of us. So Tim and I decided we're
going to roll turns and pretty much push
on limit until we can get to the front
group. So as you can see, Tim is on the
red bike in front of me and we're
rolling turns pretty much the entirety
of the way there. We did actually pick
up quite a few stragglers along the way,
too, and we did have quite a pelon
going. However, typically it was just
Tim and I that were rolling turns. Now
in Sydney, I ride with Tim quite a lot,
so we both know our strengths and
weaknesses. Every Friday, you'll
typically catch me riding with Tim. I
actually said to Tim before the ride,
"We're going to have to put out some big
WS to try and close the gap to this
group in front." And Tim laughed at me
and said, "Mate, you love putting our
watts on the flat." Which he's right. I
do. And we work very well together in
this scenario. As you can see, we just
passed by someone on the right there
who's had a crash. On this day, we saw
numerous crashes. It was quite wet on
the roads and very, very slippery. Some
people hit some white lines which you
should definitely avoid when you are
chopping off in the wet or even in the
wet in general. Tim rolls through at the
front here after I've done my turn and
we could see a big group ahead of us
here which we were trying to bridge to
again. We do make the bridge to the
group in front and as you can see Tim
keeps turning back to look at me. Kind
of questioning what we want to do here.
Sit with this group or push on to the
group in front. Now we knew that this
was not the front pelon and we knew we
had a little bit more work to do in
order to get to the very front. So, I
said, you know, let's keep on going and
we'll push through. We'll hopefully grab
some boys out of this group who'll roll
some turns with us. Tim jumped on my
wheel here and we push our way to the
front of this group. Once we got to the
front of this group, I was actually
quite eager to sit in and rest the legs
for a little bit. However, Tim had
another thing on his mind and he wanted
to bridge to the group in front of them.
I actually say to Tim at this point,
there is a massive bridge to do here,
mate, and it's going to be a hard
effort. But Tim was on a mission and was
going for it. He did an absolutely
monstrous effort on the front and I
think he was on the front for a total
minute which gave me some time to let my
legs recover. On the right side of Tim's
leg, you can actually see the pelaton
ahead of us which motivated myself and I
went, "All right, I've sat on Tim's
wheel for enough time now. I've let my
legs recover. I'm going to give him a
break and I know that I can bridge the
gap to the group in front. Given it was
flat, that is probably where I am most
optimal. I can roll quite fast on the
flat given I have a bit more weight than
most people and I had some power to put
out. I roll past tin there on the right
and then we are pretty much coming up to

videos.curation_reason: High-quality race day experience showing the chaos and challenges of the wet 2025 race with specific power and speed data.

videos.title: Tour De Brisbane 2025 - 120km Gran Fondo! UCI Event
videos.description:
Had a crack at the Tour De Brisbane this year. It was a super fun event and will definitely be doing it again next year! 

#auscycling #UCI #cycling #roadcycling #brisbane #Tourdebrisbane #gopro #granfondo 

00:00 - Race Start
04:05 - Story Bridge 
06:08 - Descending into bus tunnel 
06:50 - Eleanor Schonell Bridge
08:10 - Bonking on Coot-Tha
08:35 - Mt Coot-Tha Descent
11:05 - Riding along the water 
11:40 - Finish through bus tunnels

videos.transcript:
[Music]
foreign
since 2019 has taken us all over
Brisbane after fantastic feedback from
the 2023 event in 2024 we're proud to
return to a much-loved course continuing
the tradition of riding our city like
you've never ridden before
beginning your ride at the Brisbane
showgrounds Riders will dip straight
into the inner Northern busway at
Hurston station riding under the city to
Queen Street
from there it's around the treasury
casino and through the CBD right through
the city's heart
we will continue over the iconic Story

onto the southeast busway at South Bank
Riders will enjoy the dedicated bus

videos.title: Tour de Brisbane Gran Fondo   Part 1
videos.description: My first attempt at the Tour de Brisbane Gran Fondo.  I am an average rider who is a bit heavy so not so fast on climbs, great scenery, bus tunnels, Legacy tunnel, Story Bridge, Brisbane River

quotes.text: Something like 7,000 people made getting to the start line absolute chaos. Rounding through a capital city and juggling logistics of thousands of riders across multiple distances and age groups made for some pretty sketchy, twisty sections littered with road furniture.

From the get-go, we were already hundreds of riders back and had to make some serious ground. This ride was absolute carnage. We averaged 330 normalized watts for the entirety of the ride, which was 3 hours long, chopping off through the tunnels of Brisbane City.
The Mount Cuther climb is only about 2.5 km with an average gradient of 7%. It's going to be an allout effort. Other than that, the rain added one more plan and that was to stay upright.

key_challenges.name: The Danger Zone
key_challenges.description: Sketchy, twisty sections littered with road furniture while rounding through the capital city. Numerous crashes occur here, especially on wet white lines. Riders describe it as the hardest part to escape alive.

key_challenges.name: Mount Cutha Climb
key_challenges.description: Only about 2.5 km with an average gradient of 7%, but it's an all-out effort and the primary selection point. Goes anti-clockwise with the tougher ascent following a longer gradual descent.

key_challenges.name: Brisbane City Tunnels
key_challenges.description: Riders found themselves chopping off through the tunnels of Brisbane City while chasing wheels. Multiple tunnel sections including Legacy Way tunnel make positioning difficult.

terrain_notes.text: Roads were very moist from rain over previous days, making conditions very slippery. White lines should definitely be avoided when riding in wet conditions as they cause crashes.
The speedway sections are long, flat, and smooth, running in and out of tunnels and through bus terminals, bookended by hairpin double backs that make it very hard to move around the bunch.

race_day_tips.text: Get to the front even if it means being a jerk. Starting far back means serious ground to make up, requiring big watts on the flat to bridge gaps.
Hold the front pack until the climb at about 70km in, then aim for around 5 watts per kilo on the Mount Cutha climb and hope to crest with a group who will work together.
Rolling turns with a partner works very well for bridging to front groups. Find someone whose strengths complement yours for the flat sections.

additional_quotes.text: Something like 7,000 people made getting to the start line absolute chaos.
We averaged 330 normalized watts for the entirety of the ride, which was 3 hours long.
In the days leading up to the race, my daughter had tested positive for influenza A. Although my own tests were negative, I could feel the effects of my immune response.

rider_intel.search_text: Tour de Brisbane is a 68-mile metropolitan Grand Fondo showcasing Brisbane City, its parks, and bridges across the river. The course features three main sections: dangerous twisty urban sections with road furniture that riders call the danger zone, long flat speedway sections on closed motorways running through tunnels and bus terminals with hairpin turnarounds, and the Mount Cutha climb at 70km - a 2.5km ascent averaging 7% gradient that serves as the primary selection point. Wet conditions make the course especially treacherous, with numerous crashes occurring on slippery white lines. The race attracts around 7,000 participants, creating chaotic start conditions. Key infrastructure includes multiple trips through the Legacy Way tunnel, rides over the Story Bridge and Centenary Bridge, and sections on dedicated busways. Riders report needing to average high normalized power for the 3-hour duration, with successful finishers requiring around 5 watts per kilo on the climb to stay with working groups.
